ontology.foodex2_description: Lamb meat dish, cooked, with spices and sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Lamb Andhara is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of braised lamb pieces in a spiced yoghurt-tomato gravy, usually accompanied by rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Andhara — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lamb Andhara (cr_id G00010905). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb curry analogues (USDA SR Legacy #17003 cooked lamb shoulder) as a supplementary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on lamb curry with mixed vegetables and yoghurt
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lamb Andhara. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with yoghurt and vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are onion, tomato, and yoghurt — all low-GI ingredients. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (6.29 g/100g × 250 g serving = 15.7 g) / 100 = 5.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 308 mg/100g — borderline high; partially offset by potassium 254 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 300 mg/100g, avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes, and rely on the dish's potassium (254 mg/100g) from tomatoes and onions to partially offset sodium's pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar (1.57 g/100g), moderate carbohydrate (6.29 g/100g), high protein (8.19 g/100g), low estimated GI (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb Andhara is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low sugar content, moderate carbohydrate load, and high protein which supports glycaemic control; pair with a low-GI accompaniment such as cauliflower rice or a small portion of basmati rice rather than large servings of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb (~2.8 g/100g estimated) and cholesterol (29 mg/100g); sodium 308 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Lamb Andhara in moderate portions (≤150 g), request reduced oil and salt in preparation, and note that the dish's anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provide polyphenol benefit but do not fully offset the saturated fat and sodium burden of regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Lamb Andhara as the arachidonic acid content of lamb directly amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; consider substituting with an Andhra-style chicken or fish preparation, and monitor individual response to tomatoes (nightshade) which may exacerbate joint symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Andhara is a slow-braised South Indian lamb curry originating from Nellore in Andhra Pradesh, India, and widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants. The dish features bone-in or boneless lamb pieces marinated and cooked in a rich yoghurt-tomato gravy spiced with turmeric, red chilli, ginger, garlic, curry leaves, and garam masala. The cooking process involves slow braising that tenderises the lamb and allows the spices to deeply penetrate the meat, producing a thick, aromatic, mildly tangy gravy. The texture is tender and succulent with a bold, complex spice profile — moderately hot, earthy, and slightly sour from the yoghurt and tomatoes. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 117 kcal, 8.19 g protein, 6.39 g fat, and 6.29 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 308 mg and potassium at 254 mg. The estimated glycemic index is low (~35), making it suitable for glycaemic management when paired with appropriate accompaniments. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low sugar and moderate carbohydrate content, borderline for hypertension due to sodium at 308 mg/100g, moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from lamb, and unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to the arachidonic acid content of red meat. Anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric and ginger provide polyphenol benefit. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is typically served at lunch or dinner with rice, roti, or naan.
common_user_queries: Is Lamb Andhara suitable for diabetics?
Is Lamb Andhara high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Lamb Andhara (cr_id G00010905) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb analogues and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb shoulder data (USDA #17003) applied proportionally to the fat content reported by HPB; the HPB record does not disaggregate fat into saturated/unsaturated fractions for this dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this traditional Andhra lamb preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Lamb Andhara exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status assumed based on lamb preparation in an Indian Muslim culinary tradition; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 5. The dish is described as 'Lamb Andhara' in the HPB source; it is also known regionally as Nellore lamb curry.
